title: Verify SSE survives DigitalOcean's router (App Platform go-live blocker)
description: The whole product is one long-lived text/event-stream per viewer. The Caddy deployment needs 'flush_interval -1' to stop the proxy buffering the stream to death; on App Platform we do not own the edge and cannot set that knob. Unverified: (a) whether DO's router streams text/event-stream unbuffered, (b) whether it reaps an idle-ish connection on a request/idle timeout. If either is wrong, the map silently never updates or drops every N seconds — and no test in this repo can catch it, because it is a property of their infrastructure. VERIFY BY: deploying with ADSB_SOURCE=replay (no feeder needed) and watching /api/stream for >5 minutes for ~1Hz frames. The app already sends Cache-Control: no-cache + X-Accel-Buffering: no (adsb.stream.sse/headers) and a 15s heartbeat, which is everything we can do from our side.
acceptance_criteria: Frames observed arriving at ~1Hz over a deployed App Platform URL, continuously, for at least 5 minutes.
close_reason: VERIFIED against the live deployment 2026-07-12 (live feeder, not replay). 20 s of 'curl -N https://map.bwawan.com/api/stream': one snapshot then an update every ~1.0 s, continuously — no buffering, no batching, no idle-timeout kill. The full chain is browser -> Cloudflare (bwawan.com zone) -> DigitalOcean's edge (itself Cloudflare) -> container, and SSE survives BOTH proxy layers unmodified. Same clean cadence straight at the origin. The feared managed-router buffering does not happen; the Caddy fallback recorded on this bead is not needed.

comments.author: Brandon Correa
comments.text:
Knowledge rescued from the Caddyfile before it was deleted (adsb-kh4.7 — the compose+Caddy path is retired; App Platform is the only deployment).

WHAT AN SSE STREAM NEEDS FROM A PROXY, and therefore what to test DO's router for:

1. NO RESPONSE BUFFERING. A proxy that buffers text/event-stream holds frames until its buffer fills, and the map freezes while every health check stays green. Caddy needed an explicit 'flush_interval -1' on the reverse_proxy — it does NOT reliably infer this from Content-Type: text/event-stream. Assume DO's router needs the equivalent and CANNOT be configured, which is exactly why this bead is a go-live blocker rather than a config task.
2. NO COMPRESSION OF THE STREAM. Caddy's 'encode zstd gzip' had to EXCLUDE text/event-stream, or the encoder wraps the stream and frames arrive only when the compressor flushes.
3. NO IDLE TIMEOUT SHORTER THAN THE HEARTBEAT. adsb.stream.broadcast sends a heartbeat; if the platform's idle timeout is shorter, it kills healthy connections and the browser sees a reconnect loop.

HOW TO TEST without a feeder: deploy with ADSB_SOURCE=replay and hold 'curl -N https://<app>/api/stream' open. Frames must arrive ~1/s CONTINUOUSLY. The failure is not an error — it is a silence, or a burst of batched frames, and it looks fine in the logs.

IF DO's ROUTER BUFFERS SSE and cannot be told not to, the fallback is a droplet with a proxy we control. The working Caddy config (flush_interval -1, encode excluding text/event-stream, TLS, XFF) is recoverable at:
    git show 56c85c1:Caddyfile
    git show 56c85c1:compose.yaml

Rewrite for this stack: JVM secrets/env, SSRF on the configurable ingest URL, unsafe read-string/EDN, Reagent XSS via dangerouslySetInnerHTML, CORS on the SSE endpoint, MapLibre tile-provider key handling, and Clojure dependency auditing.

That is now false: ultrafeeder's aircraft.json is the primary untrusted input, plus the HTTP API and SSE stream. Build the doc around Malli schemas in the shared .cljc domain validating at ingest.

title: The Stack has no touch identity channel — hit-slop and scrub-to-name
description:
The Stack names a tick on HOVER. Phones do not hover. So on a phone every tick is anonymous
until you manage to hit it — and a ruler tick is 3px wide (the touch guideline is 44px), a
shelf dot 7px. This is the deeper mobile inequity behind the squished-ruler report: even with
the ruler restored to full width (see the count-chip bead), 60 ticks across ~280px cannot be
poked at.

TWO MOVES (option 3 from the design conversation).

1. HIT-SLOP. Each ruler tick gets a transparent ::before that expands its hit area to the
   ruler's full cross-axis and ~+/-6px along the altitude axis. The ink stays 3px; the target
   does not.

2. SCRUB. Press and drag along the ruler: a readout follows your finger, naming the tick
   nearest your position and lighting it on the map — a chart crosshair, not a poke target.
   Release selects it. This REUSES the existing identity channel rather than inventing one:
   the scrub dispatches [:aircraft/hover icao], and a hovered tick is already named by the
   tick component and already published app-wide. No new view state.

   The stance comes from geometry, not a second media query: the ruler is horizontal when its
   rect is wider than tall. The same gesture therefore works on the desktop's vertical ruler.

This is what makes the phone BETTER than the desktop rather than merely equal: it turns a
crowded ruler into something you read THROUGH instead of aim AT.

Pure and testable: axis-pct (rect + client x/y -> position on the altitude axis, clamped)
and nearest-tick (roster + pct -> the aircraft) are pure functions; the handlers are a thin
shell over them.

NOTE: the browser suite's page does not load app.css (adsb-giu), so a DOM test of the scrub
must size the ruler inline — a zero-size rect has no axis, and axis-pct must return nil for
one rather than dividing by zero.

DONE WHEN
- A tap anywhere near a tick selects it; a drag along the ruler names ticks continuously.
- Works on both stances (horizontal phone ruler, vertical desktop ruler).
- Browser tests cover the pure math and the gesture. bb lint, bb test green.

title: [bug] Phone Stack: the shelves starve the ruler to nothing — count chips + tap-to-name sheet
description:
MEASURED at 390x844 against the running app (headless Chromium, real layout):

  live sky (58 airborne, 0 gnd, 17 no-alt):  ruler  94px | GND 44px | NO ALT 216px
  +6 gnd, +4 no-alt:                         ruler  16px
  +15 gnd, +10 no-alt:                       ruler   2px

The ruler does not degrade — it goes to zero, taking every airborne aircraft with it.

MECHANISM (adsb.css.stack/phone). In the recumbent row layout the ruler is 'flex: 1' —
flex-basis 0 — so it only ever receives LEFTOVER space. The shelves are 'flex: 0 1 auto',
so their basis is their max-content width (every dot on one line). Once the shelves exceed
the row there is no leftover, and the ruler's shrink floor is ~0 because all its children
are absolutely positioned: it has no min-content width to defend itself with. The shelves
win by construction. Desktop has the same latent starvation on the vertical axis; 700px of
height hides it today.

FIX, per the design conversation (option 2). A field of identical dots is a bad way to spend
a phone's scarcest axis: thirty grey dots communicate exactly one fact — THIRTY — and charge
200px for it. On phone each shelf becomes a fixed-width count chip (GND 14 / NO ALT 22); the
dot cluster hides. The ruler recovers ~280px. Tapping a chip opens a sheet above the Stack
that NAMES its residents (callsign, tappable to select) — which the desktop dot cluster
cannot do, so the phone gains a channel rather than losing one.

Semantics are untouched (design-direction §9): the shelf residents are still placed NOWHERE
on the altitude scale. Absent is not zero.

DONE WHEN
- Phone: ruler length is independent of shelf population; a busy sky cannot shrink it.
- Chips carry the count; tap opens/closes a sheet naming the residents; a sheet tick fires
  the existing [:aircraft/select icao] contract.
- Desktop stance unchanged (dots).
- Browser tests cover it. bb lint, bb test green.

title: bb test doesn't build CSS: app.css is absent on a fresh clone, breaking the font-src test (and the app)
description:
Surfaced 2026-07-13 by the in-progress Garden CSS migration. resources/public/app.css is now GITIGNORED build output, generated from src/css/adsb/ by 'bb css' (clojure -M:css). But nothing builds it before tests: bb test -> [test:clj test:cljs], and test:clj is a bare 'clojure -X:test' with no :depends [css]. CI (.github/workflows/ci.yml) runs 'bb test' with no 'bb css' step either.

CONSEQUENCE: on a fresh clone (i.e. CI), resources/public/app.css does not exist. Two things break:
  1. adsb.http.security-test/the-policy-matches-the-fonts-the-app-actually-loads slurps (io/resource "public/app.css") to check every @font-face URL is same-origin and on the classpath. io/resource returns nil -> slurp nil -> NPE. This test is mine (commit bbdfad5); the coupling to build output is the landmine.
  2. More importantly the APP serves no stylesheet until app.css is built — a build/deploy ordering bug, not just a test one. The Dockerfile's 'bb build' does depend on css (bb.edn line ~113), so the JAR is fine; but 'bb dev' and 'bb test' assume app.css is already there.

It passes LOCALLY only because app.css exists as leftover/watched build output. Classic works-on-my-machine.

FIX OPTIONS (owner's call — this touches the Garden migration's build wiring):
  a) test:clj :depends [css] (and/or a 'bb css' step in CI before 'bb test'). Cleanest: the resource the app serves exists whenever the app is tested. CSS compile is fast.
  b) Make the font test build/require the Garden output in-process, or read @font-face from the src/css namespace instead of the built file. Keeps the test self-contained but couples it to Garden internals.
  Recommend (a) — the ordering belongs in the build, not hacked into one test.
acceptance_criteria: A fresh clone can run 'bb test' green with no manual 'bb css' first; CI builds CSS before tests; the font-src test reads a stylesheet that reliably exists.

title: Compile CSS with Garden; app.css becomes build output
description:
Swap the hand-authored resources/public/app.css (1190 lines) for a Garden-compiled stylesheet.

Decisions (user, 2026-07-13):
- app.css becomes generated build output, gitignored, mirroring resources/public/js. Garden source is the single source of truth.
- Faithful 1:1 port. CSS custom properties (--magenta etc.) and the prefers-color-scheme night edition STAY as real CSS custom properties: the runtime cascade and the MapLibre theme (adsb.map.theme) key off the same media query. No Clojure-level token indirection.

Library: com.lambdaisland/garden (release of noprompt/garden master).

Scope: :css alias + src/css source path (garden must NOT ship in the production uberjar), port of all sections incl. 4 @font-face / 4 @keyframes / 3 @media, bb css + bb css:watch tasks, wiring into bb dev / bb build / Dockerfile, .gitignore, and an equivalence check against the current CSS.
close_reason:
Done and verified. src/css (Garden) -> resources/public/app.css, gitignored build output. Ported all 127 rules; a rule-by-rule diff against the old hand-written stylesheet is IDENTICAL (same rules, order, declarations, declaration order), and a Playwright pixel diff of the rendered chrome in light+dark x desktop+phone is identical except the live clock's digits. bb css / bb css:watch; wired into bb dev, bb build, Dockerfile. Garden and org.flatland/ordered are :css/:test alias deps only - verified 0 garden classes in target/adsb.jar.

Landmine found and fixed: garden rebuilds declaration maps with (reduce assoc (empty m) m), and a >8-pair map literal is already a hash map at read time. That silently reordered declarations - .adsb-alert's 'border: none' landed AFTER 'border-bottom' (erasing the NOTAM row rules) and .adsb-edge-arrow's 'font: inherit' AFTER 'font-weight' (erasing its bold). Fixed with adsb.css.decl/decl (flatland ordered-map, survives garden's rebuild). adsb.css-test guards it and genuinely fails if decl is reverted.

title: Cloudflare zone SSL mode is 'Full' — move to 'Full (strict)'
description:
Confirmed by the Overseer 2026-07-12: the bwawan.com zone is on 'Full', not 'Full (strict)'.

'Full' encrypts the Cloudflare -> origin hop but performs NO certificate validation — it accepts any certificate, including a self-signed one. So it stops a passive eavesdropper and does nothing against an active attacker on that path: anyone who can intercept or redirect Cloudflare -> DigitalOcean can terminate TLS with a forged cert and rewrite the response wholesale.

WHY IT MATTERS HERE SPECIFICALLY: the whole point of adsb-kh4.7 was moving the security headers INTO the app so no edge could drop them. That defence assumes the response the browser gets is the response the app sent. Under 'Full' an attacker on the origin hop authors the entire response — their own HTML, their own CSP. A policy cannot protect a page the attacker wrote. It is not a coordinate leak (the app never serves the position), but it is map integrity, and the browser shows a padlock throughout.

NOTHING BLOCKS THE FIX — measured: the origin presents a VALID, publicly-trusted certificate.
    host:   adsb-map-production-csszk.ondigitalocean.app
    issuer: Google Trust Services (CN=WE1), SAN: ondigitalocean.app, *.ondigitalocean.app
    openssl verify return code: 0 (ok)
The usual reason to be stuck on 'Full' — a self-signed or mismatched origin cert — does not apply.

CAUTIONS:
  * The setting is ZONE-WIDE. Any other proxied hostname in bwawan.com whose origin has a bad cert will start failing.
  * The feeder is NOT affected: adsb.bwawan.com is a Cloudflare Tunnel, and tunnel origins do not use this setting (cloudflared brings its own TLS).
  * Reversible in seconds. A validation failure surfaces as a loud 526, not a silent degradation.

docs/security-checklist.md and the README already require Full (strict); the deployment simply does not match them yet.
acceptance_criteria: The bwawan.com zone is set to Full (strict); map.bwawan.com still serves 200 (no 526); the feeder tunnel is unaffected.
close_reason: Overseer flipped the bwawan.com zone to Full (strict) 2026-07-12. Verified live: map.bwawan.com still serves (302 -> /index.html, the normal index redirect) and /api/stream opens 200 and holds. No 526, so Cloudflare validates the origin cert (Google Trust Services, *.ondigitalocean.app, chain OK). The feeder tunnel is unaffected as expected.

title: Per-IP SSE cap is inert in production: the client key scatters across proxy addresses
description:
MEASURED against the live deployment 2026-07-12, not inferred.

Five concurrent SSE connections from ONE client IP, against ADSB_SSE_MAX_PER_IP=4:
  via https://map.bwawan.com/api/stream                    -> 200 200 200 200 200
  via https://adsb-map-production-csszk.ondigitalocean.app -> 200 200 200 200 200
The 5th must have been 503 + Retry-After. It was not. The cap does not bind, on either path.

So the app is keying each connection under a DIFFERENT address. Note this is the OPPOSITE of the failure we predicted and documented (everyone bucketing under one proxy address and the 5th stranger on earth getting a 503). It fails the other way: nobody shares a bucket, so an anonymous client can hold open as many streams as it likes and only the total cap (ADSB_SSE_MAX_CLIENTS=100) binds. Availability is fine; the abuse control is not.

MECHANISM (unconfirmed — this is the part that needs instrumenting): the real chain is browser -> Cloudflare (bwawan.com zone) -> DigitalOcean's edge, which is ITSELF Cloudflare (server: cloudflare + cf-ray on the *.ondigitalocean.app hostname) -> container. With ADSB_TRUSTED_PROXY_HOPS=1 the app takes the RIGHTMOST X-Forwarded-For entry, which is whatever address the last proxy appended — evidently drawn from a large pool that varies per connection. Same scattering with the zone bypassed, so it is DO's edge, not ours.

Cannot be settled from outside: the app never logs the XFF it received. NEXT STEP is one diagnostic deploy — log the raw X-Forwarded-For, CF-Connecting-IP, and socket peer at SSE connect (behind an env flag, coordinates-style privacy care: these are visitor IPs, so log once and turn it off), deploy, hit it from a known address, read 'doctl apps logs', then delete the flag.

LIKELY FIX: key on CF-Connecting-IP, which Cloudflare sets to the true client and strips from client-supplied requests — a single unambiguous value with no hop arithmetic. But it is only trustworthy once adsb-wrx locks the origin; while strangers can hit the container directly, every header is forgeable.

Also rule out the boring explanation during the same deploy: confirm ADSB_SSE_MAX_PER_IP actually reached the running process.
acceptance_criteria: The container's view of the client address is measured from a real request (not inferred); the client-IP source is fixed so that N+1 concurrent connections from one address get 503 + Retry-After, proven with curl against the live app; broadcast_test covers the chosen header; docs updated.

comments.author: Brandon Correa
comments.text:
IMPORTANT 2026-07-13 — the CF-Connecting-IP fix (commit 0814925) is UNVERIFIED and may not work, because the topology is Cloudflare-in-front-of-Cloudflare.

.ondigitalocean.app resolves to Cloudflare IPs: DO fronts App Platform with its own Cloudflare, so the real chain is browser -> OUR Cloudflare (bwawan.com zone) -> DO's Cloudflare -> container. This double-CF is the likely reason the per-IP cap never bound.

The open question my fix depends on: what CF-Connecting-IP does the INNER (DO's) Cloudflare write by the time the container reads it? If it preserves the real eyeball, the fix works. If it overwrites with our outer-CF edge address (which rotates across CF's pool), the fix fails the SAME way hop-counting did — or worse, buckets all real users under a few addresses and starts locking THEM out at max-per-ip. Cannot be inferred; I have been wrong reasoning about proxy behavior twice this session.

RESOLUTION unchanged and now mandatory before closing: the one-deploy diagnostic. Log raw CF-Connecting-IP + X-Forwarded-For + socket peer at SSE connect (env-flagged, visitor IPs so log once and remove), deploy, hit from a known address, read 'doctl apps logs', delete the flag. That single line says whether the fix is real.

Note the total cap (ADSB_SSE_MAX_CLIENTS) is unaffected by this and, once adsb-wrx lands, becomes the solid protection: only real Cloudflare traffic reaches the container, so outsiders cannot exhaust the 100 slots regardless of how the per-IP key resolves.

comments.author: Brandon Correa
comments.text:
VERIFIED STILL BROKEN 2026-07-13, with BOTH fixes live (CF-Connecting-IP keying from commit 0814925, and the origin token). Five concurrent SSE from my single IP through map.bwawan.com, cap=4:  200 200 200 200 200. The 5th must have been 503. The per-IP cap does not bind.

So the CF-Connecting-IP fix did NOT work — exactly the outcome I hedged for. The container is keying each connection under a different address, which means CF-Connecting-IP as the container receives it is NOT my single real IP. This is the double-Cloudflare topology biting: our zone in front of DO's own Cloudflare, and the inner CF is evidently not preserving the real eyeball IP by the time the container reads it (or the header is absent and the code falls through to X-Forwarded-For / socket peer, both of which scatter).

Cannot be diagnosed further from outside — no endpoint echoes the client address. The diagnostic (ADSB_DIAGNOSE_CLIENT_IP, now DEPLOYED but off) is the only way forward:
  1. DO panel: set ADSB_DIAGNOSE_CLIENT_IP=true, redeploy.
  2. Open the stream from a known address (phone on cellular is ideal — a clean single IP).
  3. doctl apps logs <app-id> --type run | grep SSE-CLIENT-IP-DIAG
  4. Read which of {cf-connecting-ip, x-forwarded-for, socket-peer} equals the known address. THAT is the header to key on. If NONE is stable per-visitor, per-IP fairness is not achievable under double-CF and we fall back to the total cap (which is now solid — adsb-wrx closed the skip-CF door) plus optional Cloudflare edge rate-limiting.
  5. Unset the flag, redeploy.
(I cannot read DO logs from here — doctl is not authed in this environment — so step 3 is yours, or paste the lines to me.)

title: Origin is publicly reachable and accepts forged X-Forwarded-For — lock it to Cloudflare
description:
Measured 2026-07-12 against the live deployment.

https://adsb-map-production-csszk.ondigitalocean.app serves the app directly, bypassing the bwawan.com Cloudflare zone entirely: GET /index.html -> 200, and GET /healthz with a forged 'X-Forwarded-For: 1.2.3.4' -> 200. Anyone on the internet can reach the container and hand it any XFF they like.

WHY THIS IS LOAD-BEARING: ADSB_TRUST_FORWARDED_FOR=true is only sound when every connection PROVABLY arrives through a trusted proxy (adsb.stream.broadcast ns docstring). That premise is false right now. Whatever we key the per-IP cap on — rightmost XFF, CF-Connecting-IP, anything header-borne — is forgeable while the origin answers strangers directly. The lock is the load-bearing part, not the choice of header.

Note App Platform fronts *.ondigitalocean.app with Cloudflare itself (responses carry server: cloudflare + cf-ray), so 'behind Cloudflare' is NOT the same as 'behind OUR Cloudflare'.

OPTIONS (need to pick one):
  a) A shared secret header. Cloudflare Transform Rule adds e.g. X-Origin-Token: <random> on requests to the origin; a Ring middleware 403s anything without it. Cheap, works on any plan, no IP list to maintain. The secret is an app-level SECRET, never committed.
  b) DO Trusted Sources / IP allowlist of Cloudflare's published ranges, if available on this app tier. Ranges change; needs periodic refresh.
Until one lands, treat the per-IP cap as advisory and the XFF as untrusted.
acceptance_criteria: The .ondigitalocean.app hostname (or any non-Cloudflare path to the container) returns non-2xx for an anonymous request, verified with curl; the app still serves normally through map.bwawan.com; and the mechanism is documented in security-checklist.md.
close_reason:
VERIFIED against the live deployment 2026-07-13 after the origin token was set (CF Transform Rule stamps X-Origin-Token; ADSB_ORIGIN_TOKEN encrypted secret on DO).

  https://adsb-map-production-csszk.ondigitalocean.app/           -> 403 (was 200)
  ...same host /index.html, /api/stream                            -> 403
  ...same host /healthz                                            -> 200 (exempt, so DO's health check still passes — the container is not killed)
  https://map.bwawan.com/  -> 302, /healthz -> 200                  (real path unaffected)

The skip-Cloudflare door (the .ondigitalocean.app hostname) is shut. The container now answers only requests that carry the secret, which only our Cloudflare zone stamps.

CORRECTION to an earlier claim on this bead: the 'Host-spoof to a DO Cloudflare IP with Host: map.bwawan.com' I described as a SECOND open door was a misread. Re-tested with the lock on, that path is SERVED (302 / streaming), i.e. it carries the token — because Cloudflare routes by SNI: presenting SNI=map.bwawan.com to ANY Cloudflare edge IP is handled by the bwawan.com zone, Transform Rule and all. So it was never a bypass; it was the legitimate path reached via a different anycast IP. The only true bypass was the .ondigitalocean.app hostname (different zone, no Transform Rule), and that is what is now 403. DO fronts everything with Cloudflare and exposes no raw origin IP, so there is no non-Cloudflare path left to the container.

comments.author: Brandon Correa
comments.text:
MEASURED 2026-07-13: a platform redirect on the .ondigitalocean.app domain would NOT close this — there is a second door.

.ondigitalocean.app resolves to Cloudflare IPs (162.159.140.98, 172.66.0.96): DO fronts App Platform with its OWN Cloudflare, separate from the bwawan.com zone (104.21.x). Knowing that origin IP, a Host-spoof reaches the container directly, bypassing our Cloudflare zone entirely:
    curl --resolve map.bwawan.com:443:162.159.140.98 https://map.bwawan.com/healthz    -> 200
    curl --resolve map.bwawan.com:443:162.159.140.98 https://map.bwawan.com/api/stream -> SSE opens, frames flow
A redirect is keyed on hostname; this request claims the SERVED hostname (map.bwawan.com), not the redirected one, so the rule never fires. The attacker knows the IP because it is what .ondigitalocean.app resolves to.

WHY THE ORIGIN TOKEN STILL WINS: it is enforced by the container on every request regardless of Host/IP/door. The Host-spoof path skips our Cloudflare zone, so the Transform Rule never stamps the token, so the container 403s it. Same door the redirect leaves open; the token shuts it. Fails closed vs the redirect's fails open.

SIDE FINDING: DO's Cloudflare returns 403 when a CLIENT tries to set CF-Connecting-IP (so it is not client-forgeable), but a forged X-Forwarded-For still gets 200. Recorded for adsb-nnk.

title: Harden the feeder tunnel: scope ingress, service-token-only Access, verify receiver.json is gated
description:
The Cloudflare Tunnel protects the receiver position, and it only does so if the Access policy is right. The tunnel closes the NETWORK leak (no home IP in public DNS, no forwarded port); it does NOT close the DATA leak — feeder.bwawan.com/data/receiver.json IS the antenna's lat/lon, and /data/aircraft.json carries r_dst/r_dir, which one aircraft position inverts to the exact antenna location. An ungated tunnel hostname is a CDN-accelerated port-forward.

Three things to get right at go-live, none of which the code can enforce:

1. INGRESS IS ONE SERVICE WIDE. The cloudflared config forwards feeder.bwawan.com to the ultrafeeder host:port only, with a http_status:404 catch-all last. A tunnel is a hole in the home network; it should be the width of the one service that needs it, not the width of the LAN.
2. SERVICE-TOKEN-ONLY ACCESS POLICY. Action 'Service Auth' (not Allow), whose ONLY Include rule is the service token. No email/identity principal alongside it — otherwise a phished Cloudflare account reaches the feeder. The cloud app is the only holder of the token (ADSB_FEEDER_AUTH_ID/SECRET, both-or-neither at boot).
3. VERIFY receiver.json IS GATED, not just aircraft.json. Unauthenticated GET of BOTH /data/receiver.json and /data/aircraft.json must be non-2xx (302 to login or 403). If receiver.json ever answered 200 while the tunnel was up, treat the position as DISCLOSED — you cannot know who read it — and decide whether to keep publishing the map.

Also rotate the service token if it was ever committed: amending the commit does not unleak it.

README 'Cloud-to-home feeder tunnel' documents all of this with the verification commands (adsb-kh4.7 session).
acceptance_criteria: cloudflared ingress scoped to the feeder service with a 404 catch-all; Access policy is Service Auth with the service token as its only principal; unauthenticated GET of /data/receiver.json AND /data/aircraft.json both return non-2xx, verified with curl against the live tunnel and the output recorded on this bead.

comments.author: Brandon Correa
comments.text:
PARTIAL VERIFICATION 2026-07-12, against the live tunnel (feeder is at adsb.bwawan.com, not feeder.bwawan.com — spec and README corrected).

PASSES — the criterion that mattered most: unauthenticated GET returns 403 for BOTH
    https://adsb.bwawan.com/data/receiver.json  -> 403
    https://adsb.bwawan.com/data/aircraft.json  -> 403
Checked by status code only; bodies were never fetched, so the coordinates did not leave the feeder. The Access policy is gating the hostname. The receiver position is NOT public.

STILL UNVERIFIED (I cannot see these from outside — they are dashboard/host config):
  1. cloudflared ingress is scoped to the ultrafeeder host:port with a http_status:404 catch-all, i.e. the tunnel is one service wide and not a hole into the LAN.
  2. The Access policy's ONLY principal is the service token (action Service Auth, no email/identity rule alongside it).
A 403 proves SOMETHING is gating; it does not prove the policy has no second door.

comments.author: Brandon Correa
comments.text:
POLICY CONFIRMED by the Overseer 2026-07-12: one Access application for adsb.bwawan.com, one policy, one rule, one service token (deliberately non-expiring).

Corroborated from outside — a sweep of 11 paths (/, /data/*, /skyaware, /tar1090/, /graphs1090/, /map, /api, /favicon.ico, a query string) returned 403 on EVERY one. Two things follow: the application covers the whole hostname rather than just /data (no path-scoped gap), and there is no identity-based Allow policy alongside the token — an Allow policy would have 302'd an unauthenticated browser to Cloudflare's login page, and a Bypass policy would have returned 200. Behaviour matches a service-token-only Service Auth policy.

NON-EXPIRING TOKEN — accepted, but note the tradeoff so it is a decision and not an accident: a credential that never expires cannot be un-leaked by waiting. The compensating controls are that it lives only as a DigitalOcean app-level SECRET and never in the repo, and that a suspected leak means rotating it by hand (nothing will do it for you). If it is ever committed, pasted, or logged, rotate in Cloudflare — amending the commit does not unleak it.

STILL OPEN — the tunnel's ingress scope, which is the last item on this bead and cannot be seen from outside: exactly one public hostname (adsb.bwawan.com -> the feeder host:port) with an http_status:404 catch-all last, AND an empty 'Private Networks' tab on the tunnel (a tunnel can route a whole CIDR to the LAN for WARP clients — configured somewhere entirely different from the ingress rules, and a far bigger hole than the one hostname).

title: App Platform retarget: finish header migration, retire the Caddy path
description: Overseer decision 2026-07-12: deployment pivots from droplet+compose+Caddy to DigitalOcean App Platform (TLS terminated by the platform; .do/ spec in progress in the Overseer's working tree, along with moving security headers from the Caddyfile into adsb.http.security). This bead tracks completing that migration: headers app-side with tests (incl. adding font-src 'self' when self-hosted fonts land), .do/app spec finalized, Caddyfile+compose either deleted or demoted to documented alternative, SSE limits verified to use the platform's forwarded-for semantics correctly (the XFF trust model from adsb-kh4.4 assumed our own trusted proxy - App Platform's forwarding needs re-verification), README deployment docs retargeted, and adsb-kh4.6 (go-live) consuming the new path. NOTE: this is largely the Overseer's own active WIP - agents must not enter these files until the WIP lands or the Overseer hands it off.
acceptance_criteria: Headers emitted by the app with tests; .do spec deploys; XFF/per-IP limits correct behind App Platform; docs updated; Caddy path resolved (deleted or documented-alternative); kh4.6 runbook retargeted.
notes: font-src 'self' needed for self-hosted fonts: resources/public/fonts/ ships the §5 faces — Space Mono (400/400i/700) and Space Grotesk (500), OFL, LICENSE.md alongside — with @font-face in app.css. (The #/preview fitting room and its audition-only faces were cleared out, bead adsb-gdz.) bb dev's shadow server serves fonts without ceremony; the backend CSP blocks them until font-src 'self' lands in adsb.http.security with this header migration. Note the basemap glyphs (resources/public/glyphs/) are PBF fetched same-origin under connect-src/default-src, not font-src.

comments.author: Brandon Correa
comments.text:
Session 2026-07-12. The code migration landed under adsb-9n6 (closed); this session finished the residue.

DONE:
- font-src 'self' added to the CSP (adsb.http.security). This was a live production bug, not a nicety: the §5 faces are self-hosted in resources/public/fonts and app.css @font-face fetches them, but the policy had no font-src, so default-src 'none' governed and EVERY face would have been refused in the deployed app. Verified in real headless Chromium (Playwright) against a running server: 3 faces fetched 200, document.fonts reports them loaded, and ZERO securitypolicyviolation events. (The 4th, space-mono-400i, stays 'unloaded' — lazy, no italic text on the first view.)
- Test tied to the CSS, not to a string: security_test asserts every @font-face url in app.css is same-origin AND resolvable on the classpath, so adding a font CDN fails the suite instead of failing silently in a browser.
- Docs retargeted: README Deployment (App Platform primary + doctl commands, compose/Caddy as documented alternative, new 'The client address' section with the hop-measuring procedure), env table (+ADSB_TRUSTED_PROXY_HOPS, +ADSB_DEV_CSP), security-checklist §4/§6 (headers ship from the app not the edge; hop-count audit item; dev-CSP-can't-reach-prod item; font-src in the recited policy), validation-boundaries Boundary 2 + client-address trust model (rewrote 'rightmost entry' as the hops model).
- Caddy path resolved: demoted to documented alternative, sets no security headers (two sources of truth, where Caddy's 'header' REPLACES, would silently win over the app's).

bb test:clj green (146 tests / 630 assertions), bb lint clean.

REMAINS (needs a live deployment, cannot be done here): '.do spec deploys' and 'XFF/per-IP limits correct behind App Platform'. Both are deploy-time measurements — noted on adsb-kh4.6, and SSE-through-the-router is adsb-ju1 (P0). Suggest kh4.7 closes when kh4.6 confirms the hop count, or that criterion moves to kh4.6.

comments.author: Brandon Correa
comments.text:
Caddy path RESOLVED BY DELETION (user decision, not demotion). compose.yaml and Caddyfile are gone.

Rationale: nothing depended on them — no CI job, no bb task, .dockerignore even excluded compose.yaml from the image, and local dev is bb dev / bare 'docker run', not compose. They existed to serve a droplet deployment that isn't happening, while being a second (untested) source of truth for the JVM memory sizing and ADSB_TRUSTED_PROXY_HOPS. 'Documented alternative' is how dead config lives forever.

Rescued first: the Caddyfile's SSE-through-a-proxy knowledge (no buffering / flush_interval -1, encode must exclude text/event-stream, idle timeout vs heartbeat) is now recorded on adsb-ju1, along with 'git show 56c85c1:Caddyfile' for recovery if DO's router turns out to buffer SSE and we need a proxy we control.

Consequence worth naming: ADSB_TRUSTED_PROXY_HOPS=1 used to be a MEASURED fact (Caddy was one hop). With Caddy gone it is a GUESS about DigitalOcean's chain that nobody has counted. Docstrings, the .do spec, README, and security-checklist now all say 'guess', not 'default' — the per-IP cap is unproven until adsb-kh4.6 measures it.

Cleaned every reference: README (deployment table -> single App Platform path, 'Run the container locally', TLS/edge section, dropped ADSB_DOMAIN/ACME_EMAIL env rows), security-checklist §4, validation-boundaries Boundary 2 + client-address model, .dockerignore, .do/app.yaml, and the ns docstrings in adsb.http.security / .server / .routes, adsb.stream.broadcast, adsb.env. Historical mentions ('used to live in a Caddy config') deliberately remain — they explain WHY the headers are app-side, and no longer point at a file a reader could open.

bb test:clj green (146/630), bb lint clean.

title: Tune deployment for DigitalOcean App Platform (512MB)
description: App Platform replaces the compose+Caddy edge: it terminates TLS and routes to the container, so the Caddyfile does not run. Three things must move or change: (1) security headers (CSP/HSTS/nosniff/Referrer-Policy/Permissions-Policy) move from Caddy into the Ring stack so they ship regardless of edge; (2) the SSE per-IP cap reads the RIGHTMOST X-Forwarded-For entry, correct behind exactly one trusted proxy — if DO's edge is more than one hop, every visitor buckets under one internal IP and the 5th gets a 503, so the trusted hop count must be configurable; (3) the JVM must be told to size itself for a 512MB container (Temurin defaults to 25% MaxRAMPercentage and G1).
close_reason: Security headers moved into the Ring stack (adsb.http.security), X-Forwarded-For hop count made configurable (ADSB_TRUSTED_PROXY_HOPS), JVM tuned for a 512MB container, .do/app.yaml added. Verified end-to-end against a live replay-mode process. SSE-through-DO-edge verification split out as its own P0.

title: Internet-exposure hardening: TLS, headers, SSE limits
description: The app is internet-facing, which is beyond what docs currently assume. Reverse proxy with TLS in front of http-kit, security headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options), a cap on concurrent SSE connections and per-IP limits so anonymous browsers cannot exhaust the server, and an update to security-checklist.md and validation-boundaries.md to reflect the new exposure in the same change.
acceptance_criteria: TLS terminates at the reverse proxy; CSP, HSTS, and X-Content-Type-Options verified on responses; a concurrent-SSE cap and per-IP limit are enforced and tested; security-checklist.md and validation-boundaries.md are updated in the same change.
notes: PRIVACY (Overseer 2026-07-11): treat the receiver position as a server-side secret - record it as such in validation-boundaries.md / security-checklist.md when updating them for internet exposure. Also ensure /data endpoints of the feeder itself are not proxied through.
close_reason: TLS terminates at a Caddy sidecar (only published ports; app port unpublished); CSP/HSTS/nosniff/Referrer-Policy/Permissions-Policy verified on real responses through the proxy and the CSP verified violation-free in headless Chromium against the release build; concurrent-SSE cap (ADSB_SSE_MAX_CLIENTS=100) and per-IP cap (ADSB_SSE_MAX_PER_IP=4) enforced atomically in the stream registry with 503+Retry-After, proven with live curl connections at cap=2 incl. slot-freeing; XFF trusted only behind the proxy (rightmost entry) after finding http-kit poisons :remote-addr with leftmost XFF; receiver-position-as-secret and never-proxy-the-feeder recorded in security-checklist.md + validation-boundaries.md in the same commit (f843178). bb test + bb lint clean.

A plane squawking 7700 is a human being having the worst day of their life - the UI must not whisper it.

Added :aircraft/emergencies sub and adsb.ui.alert ribbon (role=alert, under header, names callsign/squawk/meaning, click selects, stacks multiples, clears when calm). Panel + sidebar badges now name the meaning. Tests: aircraft_test emergency? (7500/7600/7700 + kinds + 0000/absent), alert_test (appears/names/click/clears/multiple), panel+sidebar badge meaning. bb test:clj 403 assertions, bb test:cljs 420 assertions, bb lint all clean. Commit 9c17abc.

title: geo.cljc: bearing, distance, bounds + aircraft-to-GeoJSON
description: The pure shared geo module the README promises: bearing, distance, bounds math, and conversion of the aircraft picture into a GeoJSON FeatureCollection with the properties the map styling needs (track, altitude, emergency, stale). Pure cljc, no map dependency, fully unit-tested against the fixture cast.

Tests in test/cljc/adsb/geo_test.cljc pass on JVM and browser (geo-test: 9 tests/42 assertions; full browser suite 28/146 green). Lint clean for the two files. Committed 5e246da via explicit pathspec.

title: Imperative aircraft layer via GeoJSON setData
description: The architectural centerpiece: a MapLibre GeoJSON source updated via setData on every SSE tick, entirely outside React. Hundreds of aircraft at 1 Hz must not touch the Reagent render path. re-frame owns the chrome; the map owns the planes. Includes the interop seam that lets tests fake the map object per testing-setup.md.
acceptance_criteria: Aircraft updates flow through setData with zero Reagent re-renders (asserted in a test via render counting); the fake-map seam lets CLJS tests capture setData payloads; hundreds of aircraft at 1 Hz stay smooth.
close_reason: Imperative aircraft layer landed, commit c7f2672. adsb.map.aircraft-layer: attach! waits on the map's load event, adds the GeoJSON source + a NEUTRAL circle layer through the grown maplibre seam (on-load!/add-source!/add-layer!/set-source-data! — clj->js at the very edge, so tests capture Clojure data), then starts reagent.core/track! over the :aircraft/picture subscription OUTSIDE any component: one picture change = one geo/aircraft-picture->feature-collection + one setData, zero React. Zero-re-render PROOF in adsb.map.aircraft-layer-test: shell mounted for real, 25 :stream/received frames dispatched — 25 setData calls (+1 load flush), map component render count pinned at 1, mount count 1, and unmount stops pushes. Ordering hazard: frames arriving before load land in app-db (wholesale-replaced per adsb-2yu.2), so app-db IS the latest-wins buffer; load flushes the current picture exactly once — tested (2 early frames -> 1 push, latest only). Clock: stale judged at js/Date.now at push time (frame arrival) via redef-able now-ms — the imperative edge owns the clock, the domain takes it as an argument. Feature properties icao/callsign/track/altitude(incl "ground")/emergency/stale all proven to reach the source, so adsb-2yu.5 is pure style-expression work on layer-spec. Browser suite 52/265, JVM 94/370, lint clean. NOTE: bb test:cljs currently fails to connect to the long-running shadow-cljs watch (SERVER INSTANCE OUT OF DATE after a concurrent deps.edn change) — suite was run via 'npx shadow-cljs compile test --force-spawn' + the Playwright driver; the dev watch needs a restart to pick up new deps, left untouched.

comments.author: Brandon Correa
comments.text:
APP-DB CONTRACT from adsb-2yu.2 (SSE client), the read surface for the imperative map layer:

:aircraft/picture — icao(string) -> domain aircraft (namespaced :aircraft/* keys, decoded via adsb.wire/wire->picture). Subscribe with (rf/subscribe [:aircraft/picture]); {} before the first frame. Every snapshot/update frame WHOLESALE-REPLACES this key (never a merge), so this ratom is the full current sky each tick — an aircraft absent from the newest frame is simply gone. Read it OUTSIDE React: add-watch the reaction (or a re-frame/subscribe deref inside a reagent.core/track + add-on-dispose, or an fx on :stream/received) and push straight into the MapLibre GeoJSON source via setData. Do NOT mount a component per aircraft. Aircraft values may lack :aircraft/position (never-positioned targets) — those produce no map feature (adsb.geo handles the projection; GeoJSON is [lon lat]).

:stream/connection — :live | :reconnecting | :down, via (rf/subscribe [:stream/connection]). For the header indicator (adsb-dgb.3), not the map layer.

Note: the live EventSource is NOT in app-db (it is a stateful JS object held in a private atom in adsb.stream). The map layer needs only the two subs above.

title: SSE client with reconnect and connection state
description: EventSource wrapper: parse snapshot and update events into app-db aircraft state, reconnect with backoff when the stream drops, and expose connection status (live / reconnecting / down) as a re-frame sub so the chrome can show it honestly. The map should recover from a server restart without a page reload.
acceptance_criteria: Snapshot and update events land in app-db; killing the server flips the connection sub to reconnecting and recovery is automatic without a page reload; reconnect attempts back off.
close_reason: SSE client delivered. adsb.stream.source: EventSource seam (Connection protocol + connect!, redef-able like adsb.map.maplibre / adsb.ingest.source). adsb.stream: re-frame client — decodes each snapshot/update frame with the shared adsb.wire codec and WHOLESALE-REPLACES :aircraft/picture (icao -> namespaced domain aircraft; never a merge), and drives :stream/connection honestly: :reconnecting while EventSource auto-retries a CONNECTING drop, own exponential backoff (1s->30s cap, reset on open) when the source reaches CLOSED, :down surfaced past 3 attempts while still retrying — so a server restart heals with no page reload. Live EventSource lives in a private atom, not app-db; app-db holds only :aircraft/picture and :stream/connection (subs of the same names). Wired into adsb.core/init!. app-db contract for the map layer documented on adsb-2yu.4. Browser tests fake the seam (snapshot populates picture, update replaces wholesale, :live->:reconnecting->:down->:live lifecycle, backoff grows) under day8.re-frame.test isolation. bb test:cljs 45/228, bb test:clj 83/341, bb lint clean. Commit 2318c22. NOTE: src/cljc/adsb/wire.cljc shows an uncommitted reformat from a concurrent agent — left untouched, not in this commit.

title: App shell: shadow-cljs app build, MapLibre init, re-frame skeleton

The empty map that everything else lands on.

Map init behind the fakeable adsb.map.maplibre seam (create!/destroy! protocol); no-token demo-tiles basemap for DEV (prod tiles = adsb-kh4.5) with attribution; privacy-safe fixed regional center [lon -82.0, lat 28.0] zoom 7, never the receiver. CLJS test (test/cljs/adsb/map/view_test.cljs) asserts center/zoom + token-free style and that the shell mounts + inits the map through the seam. Verified for real in headless Chromium via the now-landed adsb-4ga.8 runner: 8 tests / 17 assertions, 0 fail; lint clean; live-app smoke shows canvas + attribution, 0 page errors. bb dev serves the app on :8080 via shadow-cljs :dev-http.

title: SSE fan-out of aircraft state
description: The stream the browser lives on: on connect, send a full snapshot of the current picture, then push updates at ~1 Hz. Defines the wire format (JSON from the domain model - decide and document how namespaced keys serialize). Client registry with cleanup on disconnect, and a policy for slow consumers so one stalled browser cannot back-pressure the loop or leak channels.
acceptance_criteria: A new client receives a full snapshot then ~1 Hz updates; disconnect removes the client without leaking channels; a slow-consumer policy is implemented and documented; periodic SSE heartbeat comments keep the stream alive through buffering proxies; the wire format is documented.
notes: PRIVACY (Overseer 2026-07-11): receiver position is private. The wire format must exclude the receiver position AND all receiver-relative fields (r_dst, r_dir) - they are trivially invertible to the antenna location. Add a test asserting these fields never appear in SSE payloads.
close_reason: Delivered the SSE stream + boot integration. GET /api/stream: snapshot-then-updates at ~1 Hz over http-kit async channels, ': hb' heartbeats every 15s, client registry with on-close removal, drop-and-close slow-consumer policy (full-picture frames bound the backlog). Wire format is adsb.wire (cljc, bidirectional codec, allowlist projection): flat unqualified kebab-case JSON, envelope {at, aircraft[...]}; receiver position / r_dst / r_dir / rssi can never reach the wire by construction - privacy tests over the projection AND over serialized frames from the real fixture pipeline. adsb.main/start! is the one composition root (validate feeder URL loudly, resolve receiver position once, range-gate in the poll callback per nqf.3 handoff, age-out folded into the broadcast tick, live feeder status on /healthz); :dev alias boots adsb.main so bb dev streams. Required http-kit 2.9.0-beta1 (2.8.x never detects HTTP-streaming disconnects - own commit 0d3c7f8). bb test (83 clj tests/341 assertions + 41 cljs/210) and bb lint clean. Feature commit b0b8304, scoped pathspecs only. See bead comments for the full wire spec and decisions.

comments.author: Brandon Correa
comments.text: WIRE FORMAT: see adsb.wire ns docstring (src/cljc/adsb/wire.cljc).

comments.author: Brandon Correa
comments.text:
WIRE FORMAT (the contract adsb-2yu.2 builds against; canonical doc: adsb.wire ns docstring, src/cljc/adsb/wire.cljc):
- Endpoint: GET /api/stream, Content-Type text/event-stream. On connect one 'snapshot' event, then 'update' events at ~1 Hz, ': hb' heartbeat comment every 15 s. Frames carry increasing 'id:' lines; Last-Event-ID is ignored on reconnect - every frame is the FULL picture, so a reconnect just takes a fresh snapshot.
- Event names: snapshot, update. Both carry the same JSON envelope: {"at": <epoch-ms>, "aircraft": [<wire aircraft>...]}. Updates are NOT deltas; treat every frame as a wholesale replacement.
- Wire aircraft: flat UNqualified kebab-case keys (same choice as geo.cljc feature properties), absent facts omitted, never defaulted: icao, callsign, lat, lon, altitude (ft), on-ground (true only), squawk, ground-speed (kt), track (deg), baro-rate (fpm), seen-at (epoch-ms last heard), position-suspect (true only - the nqf.3 spoofing flag, surfaced).
- The codec is cljc and bidirectional: the frontend should decode with adsb.wire/wire->picture (or wire->aircraft) so the contract cannot drift.
PRIVACY: aircraft->wire is an ALLOWLIST projection - receiver position, r_dst/r_dir, and any receiver-relative key can never reach the wire by construction. rssi is also withheld (it measures the receiver, not the aircraft) - if the sidebar ever wants signal strength, that is a deliberate decision for a new bead. Enforced by adsb.wire-test (both platforms, incl. a poisoned-aircraft test) and adsb.stream.broadcast-test/wire-privacy over real serialized HTTP frames from the fixture pipeline.

comments.author: Brandon Correa
comments.text:
SLOW-CONSUMER POLICY (documented in adsb.stream.broadcast ns docstring): DROP-AND-CLOSE. http-kit async channels do not backpressure - send! only enqueues and its boolean says whether the channel was still open - so any client whose send! returns false is closed and unregistered on the spot; on-close removes clients whose sockets die. What bounds a stalled-but-open consumer: every frame is the full picture, never a queued backlog of deltas, so the server buffers at most one bounded frame per tick until the OS gives up on the socket and send! fails. A dropped client reconnects and gets a fresh snapshot.

DEPENDENCY NOTE: http-kit bumped 2.8.0 -> 2.9.0-beta1 in its own commit (0d3c7f8). On 2.8.x, plain-HTTP-streaming close detection is broken (closeKey notifies the accept-time AsyncChannel, not the per-request one - #375 regression fixed by #578), so :on-close never fired and send! never failed: SSE clients would leak forever. Verified empirically both ways; the disconnect-cleanup test fails on 2.8.x, passes on 2.9.0-beta1. Watch for a stable 2.9.0 and bump when it lands.

OTHER DECISIONS:
- Age-out cadence folded into the broadcast tick: the broadcaster's injected picture fn is adsb.state/age-out!, which prunes AND returns the picture - one scheduler for both jobs, runs even with zero clients.
- One composition path: adsb.main/start! is the only wiring (poll -> range gate -> state -> SSE/HTTP); the :dev alias now boots adsb.main, so bb dev streams too and REQUIRES ADSB_ULTRAFEEDER_URL (fails loudly, Boundary 3). adsb.http.server/-main removed.
- nqf.3 handoff wired: receiver/resolve-position! once at boot, plausibility/gate-range in the poll callback before state/apply-batch!; the receiver position lives only in the composition closure - never state, never wire, never logged.
- healthz: feeder-status now reports the live poller status (starting/ok/down) via an injected status fn; 'unknown' only when no poller is wired (bare handler in tests).

title: HTTP server: reitit routes, Malli coercion, static assets
description: http-kit server with reitit routing: /api/aircraft/:icao with Malli-coerced path params and schema-checked responses per validation-boundaries.md Boundary 2, a health endpoint for the container orchestrator, static serving of the compiled frontend, and a start/stop lifecycle that is REPL-friendly. No hand-rolled parsing in handlers; no keyword interning of user input.
acceptance_criteria: A malformed :icao is rejected by Malli coercion with a 400 before any handler code runs; responses are schema-checked; the health endpoint reports feeder reachability; the compiled frontend is served; start/stop is idempotent from the REPL.
close_reason: HTTP server implemented under src/clj/adsb/http/: reitit router with Malli coercion on /api/aircraft/:icao (garbage icao -> 400 in middleware, no keyword interning), schema-checked responses, /healthz with stubbed feeder-status, static serving of resources/public, and idempotent start!/stop! now booted by bb dev via the :dev alias. aircraft-detail reads an injected state-lookup defaulted to empty state (404s until adsb-nqf.2). bb test:clj and bb lint clean. Commit 3f1c071.

title: Aircraft state store + staleness domain
description: The current picture of the sky. Pure cljc domain fns in adsb.aircraft: merge an incoming batch into the picture keyed by ICAO hex, stale? by seen-age with time passed as an argument (never read the clock in cljc), and age-out removal of long-silent aircraft. Backend holds the picture in one atom updated by the poll loop. The long-silent and never-positioned cast members are the tests.
acceptance_criteria: Pure cljc: merging a batch updates the picture keyed by hex; stale? and age-out take time as an argument, never reading a clock; the long-silent cast member ages out and never-positioned is retained without a position; the backend atom is updated only by the poll loop.
close_reason: Pure merge-batch/age-out/stale?/positioned? in adsb.aircraft (seen-at-ms = capture - seen; position retained across position-less updates, other absent fields go absent; retained across missed polls, aged out at 300s silence). Thin atom shell adsb.state wired as the http server's default :state-lookup. cljc+clj tests against the cast; bb test and bb lint clean. Commit b496548.

title: Source protocol + ultrafeeder polling source
description: adsb.ingest.source/Source protocol and its first implementation: poll /data/aircraft.json at ~1 Hz. ADSB_ULTRAFEEDER_URL comes from the environment only, is validated at boot (must parse, must be http/https, fail loudly at startup - see validation-boundaries.md Boundary 3). The loop backs off when the feeder is unreachable and never dies; a feeder outage is a status, not a crash.
acceptance_criteria: Boot fails loudly on a missing, malformed, or non-http(s) ADSB_ULTRAFEEDER_URL; the poll loop survives a feeder outage with visible backoff and recovers when the feeder returns; the Source protocol has a second trivial implementation (test stub) proving the seam; ingest tests replay the recorded fixture, never a live feeder.
notes: Real feeder: http://dietpi.local:8100 (ultrafeeder on dietpi.local). SBS on 30003 and Beast on 30005, same host, for the future Source swap.
close_reason: Delivered adsb.ingest.source (Source protocol open!/fetch!/close! + fn-source stub), adsb.ingest.config (pure validate-feeder-url, fails loudly naming ADSB_ULTRAFEEDER_URL), adsb.ingest.ultrafeeder (http-kit + cheshire Source feeding ->aircraft-batch), and adsb.ingest.poll (~1 Hz loop, log-once-per-state-change, exponential backoff to 30s cap, never dies, feeder status via poll/status). bb test:clj green (52 tests / 216 assertions), bb lint clean. Committed ae678d1 (only my 8 files, pathspec). RESIDUAL: poller is not wired at boot — main.clj is owned by adsb-kh4.1 and untouched here; healthz (adsb-kbm.2) + state store/SSE (adsb-nqf.2) must construct the poller, hold it, and read poll/status. See bead comment for the exact status-atom shape and callback seam.

comments.author: Brandon Correa
comments.text: Feeder status lives in the poller returned by adsb.ingest.poll/start! — read it with (adsb.ingest.poll/status poller), which derefs an atom holding {:feeder/status :starting|:ok|:down, :feeder/last-success-ms, :feeder/last-error}. For adsb-kbm.2 (/healthz) and adsb-nqf.2 (state store + SSE chrome): the poller is not yet constructed anywhere (main.clj is owned by adsb-kh4.1, untouched here). Whoever wires start! at boot should hold the returned poller and expose poll/status to the healthz handler and SSE. The on-batch! callback is the seam to the state store (adsb-nqf.2).

title: Frontend map core
description: The browser half: a MapLibre map that renders live aircraft pushed over SSE. The load-bearing rule from AUTEUR and the README applies throughout - the aircraft layer bypasses React entirely and is fed via GeoJSON setData; re-frame owns only the chrome.
acceptance_criteria: Open the app and watch live aircraft move, styled by altitude and heading, aging out honestly - with the 1 Hz path never touching React.

title: Malli schemas + ingest boundary

title: Record a real aircraft.json payload as a test fixture
description: Capture a genuine payload from the ultrafeeder and commit it to test/resources/aircraft-sample.json — warts included: the 'ground' altitude strings, the aircraft with no lat/lon, the space-padded callsigns, the nulls. Hand-written fixtures are a fiction of what we THINK the feeder sends; a recording is what it does send. notes:
Feeder confirmed live at http://dietpi.local:8100/data/aircraft.json (2026-07-11).

FIXTURE CAPTURED 2026-07-11: 10 polls at ~25s intervals, 49-52 aircraft each (348+ aircraft-observations). Richest capture (poll 10, 51 aircraft) committed as test/resources/aircraft-sample.json. PRIVACY REDACTION: r_dst and r_dir stripped from every aircraft (39 of 51 had them) via byte-preserving text surgery; verified JSON-equal to original minus exactly those keys; no receiver lat/lon fields existed in the payload. Provenance documented in test/resources/README.md.

TRAP TABLE VERIFICATION (against all 10 captures):
- alt_baro number: CONFIRMED (abc0e4 alt_baro=34775). Only int seen; no floats.
- alt_baro string 'ground': NOT OBSERVED in capture window (no airport-surface traffic in range). Claim stands, uncontradicted.
- lat/lon absent entirely: CONFIRMED (a10202, abe075, ad072c in fixture; 102 occurrences total).
- flight space-padded to 8 chars: CONFIRMED (ac5697 'SWA349  ', a5d4c3 'GTI9071 '; 397 occurrences). Also flight absent: CONFIRMED (abc0e4, adc9b6).
- hex ~-prefix (TIS-B/ADS-R): NOT OBSERVED. Only types seen: adsb_icao (292), mode_s (56). Uncontradicted.
- squawk string of 4 octal digits: CONFIRMED, always string, never int (a49498 '0745', a4008e '0541' — leading zeros prove type matters; 309 occurrences). '0000' itself not seen.
- gs/track/baro_rate absent: CONFIRMED (ac5b89 missing all three; 79/79/127 occurrences).
- null values: NOT OBSERVED (0 nulls across all captures). Claim is 'may be null' — uncontradicted.
- BONUS quirk not in table: alt_baro itself absent on 45/348 observations (abe075, a97310) — mode_s aircraft often carry only hex/messages/rssi.

NO DOC CORRECTIONS NEEDED: no trap-table claim was contradicted by the data.
close_reason: 51-aircraft real capture committed-ready at test/resources/ with r_dst/r_dir redacted (verified 0 occurrences); all trap-table quirks confirmed or explicitly not-observed; no doc corrections needed.

title: Flaky JVM test run: one bb test:clj failed, not reproducible in 8 subsequent runs
description:
Seen once on 2026-07-12, on the committed state of adsb-kh4.7 (HEAD 567a101). 'bb test:clj' exited nonzero; the Clojure error report confirmed REAL failures ('Test failures or errors occurred.' from cognitect.test-runner.api/test), not a harness glitch. The failing test NAMES were not captured — the output was piped through tail and scrolled. Eight subsequent runs (5 plain, 1 under heavy CPU load, 2 ad hoc) were all green: 146 tests / 630 assertions, 0 failures.

So: a real, intermittent failure with an unknown trigger. Do not close this by running the suite once and seeing green.

PRIME SUSPECT — shared mutable global across test namespaces. adsb.http.server holds the server in a defonce atom and start! is IDEMPOTENT: it returns the EXISTING server if one is up rather than starting a new one. THREE namespaces drive that one global — adsb.http.server-test, adsb.stream.broadcast-test (start-streaming-server!), and adsb.http.security-test (headers-survive-a-real-http-response). If any of them leaves a server up when the next starts, that next start! silently attaches to the PREVIOUS namespace's handler and dependencies, and (http-kit/server-port srv) then names the wrong server. http-kit's stop is also asynchronous while the atom is reset synchronously, so 'stopped' and 'gone' are not the same instant.

SECOND SUSPECT — broadcast-test asserts on millisecond SSE timing (interval-ms, heartbeat-ms) against a real socket; the failure happened during a burst of git commits, i.e. under load. But the suite stayed green under deliberate CPU saturation, which weakens this.

TO INVESTIGATE: run the suite in a loop capturing FULL output until it fails (do not pipe through tail), then read the failing test name. Consider making the tests not share the defonce server — an explicit start-on-ephemeral-port returning a handle, rather than a global that start! will hand back to whoever asks next.
acceptance_criteria: The failing test is identified from a captured full-output run, the mechanism explained, and the shared-server-atom hazard either fixed or ruled out with evidence. Not closed on a single green run.

title: Static asset compression on App Platform
description: Caddy gzip/zstd-compresses the ~1MB compiled frontend (encode directive, restricted to a type allowlist so it never touches text/event-stream). App Platform has no Caddy, and whether DO's edge compresses a service response is unverified. If it does not, every first load ships ~1MB of uncompressed JS. Deliberately NOT hand-rolled during adsb-9n6: a compression middleware that catches text/event-stream would buffer the SSE stream and kill the map, and doing it safely means either caching the compressed static set or excluding the stream by content-type — worth doing properly, not in passing. STEP 1 is to measure: curl -H 'Accept-Encoding: gzip' against the deployed app and read Content-Encoding.

title: Typography, scale and palette bake-off
